class_schedule.topic: What Are the Digital Humanities? Definitions, Debates, and Demonstrations
learning_objectives: Define digital humanities as a field at the intersection of computing and humanistic inquiry
Identify the major methods: text mining, network analysis, mapping, digital editions, and computational stylistics
Understand the debates: does computation change what we can know about literature, or merely how fast we can know it?
readings: Gold and Klein, Debates in the Digital Humanities, Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: The digital humanities apply computational methods to humanistic questions. For literary scholars, this means using algorithms to analyze texts at scales impossible for human readers — and confronting what is gained and lost when we trade close reading for distant reading.
key_concepts: Distant reading vs. close reading: Moretti's proposal to analyze literature at the macro scale
Text mining and topic modeling: identifying patterns across thousands of texts
Network analysis: mapping relationships between characters, authors, or texts
Digital editions: scholarly editions that exploit hypertext, annotation, and search
The debate: does computation produce new knowledge or just confirm what we already know?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live demonstration of Voyant Tools analyzing word frequency and distribution in a Shakespeare play, followed by discussion of what the tool reveals and what it cannot

review.value: Franco Moretti's term for analyzing literature not through close reading of individual texts but through computational analysis of large corpora — graphs, maps, and trees instead of words on a page
Critics argue DH merely confirms what humanists already know using expensive tools; proponents argue it reveals patterns invisible to individual readers and democratizes access to cultural heritage

class_schedule.topic: Text Analysis: Computational Approaches to Literary Style
learning_objectives: Apply text mining tools to analyze word frequency, collocation, and distribution in literary texts
Understand the basics of computational stylistics: authorship attribution and stylometric analysis
Critically evaluate what quantitative text analysis can and cannot tell us about literary meaning

lecture_notes.introduction: Computers can count words faster than humans. The interesting question is whether counting words tells us anything meaningful about literature. Computational stylistics argues that it does — and has produced surprising results in authorship attribution, genre classification, and the evolution of literary style.
key_concepts: Word frequency analysis: the most common words in a text reveal more about style than rare words
Collocation: which words appear near each other and what patterns this reveals
Stylometry: using statistical analysis of style to attribute authorship
Topic modeling: algorithms that discover thematic patterns across large text collections
The hermeneutic gap: the distance between a computational finding and a literary interpretation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running stylometric analysis on disputed Shakespeare plays to demonstrate how computational methods contribute to authorship debates
setup_requirements: Sample text corpus provided on LMS

review.value: The statistical analysis of literary style, particularly the frequency of common function words (the, of, and, to), which are unconscious stylistic markers that reliably distinguish authors
The interpretive challenge of moving from a computational result (e.g., 'this word appears 47 times') to a literary argument about meaning — computation identifies patterns but cannot interpret them

class_schedule.topic: Mapping Literature: GIS, Spatial Analysis, and Literary Geography
learning_objectives: Understand how geographic information systems can be applied to literary texts
Analyze how mapping literary settings reveals spatial patterns invisible in linear reading
Create a basic literary map using open-source tools
readings: Piatti et al., 'Mapping Literature: Towards a Geography of Fiction'

lecture_notes.introduction: Literature happens in places. Mapping those places — the settings of novels, the movements of characters, the locations of publication and readership — reveals spatial patterns that linear reading cannot detect.
key_concepts: Literary geography: the study of the relationship between literature and place
GIS for humanities: adapting geographic information systems for literary and historical data
Mapping the novel: how visualizing fictional settings reveals patterns of inclusion and exclusion
The Moretti map: mapping the marriage plots of Austen to reveal the geography of social mobility
Challenges: fictional places do not always correspond to real geography; maps imply precision that fiction does not
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a StoryMapJS project tracing the geographic movement in a novel, discussing how the map representation changes our understanding of the narrative

review.value: The interdisciplinary study of how literature represents, constructs, and is shaped by geographic space — mapping reveals which places are overrepresented and which are invisible in literary culture
Maps imply precision and factuality; fiction operates through ambiguity, metaphor, and imagination — the tension between these two modes is productive but must be acknowledged

class_schedule.topic: Digital Archives and the Politics of Access
learning_objectives: Analyze how digital archives expand access to literary and cultural heritage
Understand the politics of digitization: who decides what gets digitized, how, and for whom
Evaluate digital archives for scholarly rigor, sustainability, and ethical practice
readings: Explore: Women Writers Project, Colored Conventions Project, and Project Gutenberg

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital archives promise to democratize access to cultural heritage. But digitization is not neutral: decisions about what to digitize, how to categorize it, and who controls access reproduce existing power structures unless those choices are made deliberately and critically.
key_concepts: The promise of access: millions of texts available to anyone with an internet connection
The politics of selection: which texts get digitized and which remain invisible
Metadata as interpretation: how cataloguing decisions shape what can be found and how it is understood
Sustainability: the problem of digital decay — websites go down, formats become obsolete
Community archives: projects built by and for marginalized communities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative evaluation of three digital archives (Project Gutenberg, Women Writers Project, and Colored Conventions Project) using criteria of scholarly rigor, ethical practice, and inclusiveness

review.value: Digitization is not a neutral act of preservation — it involves choices about what is worth saving, how it is categorized, and who controls access, reproducing or challenging existing power structures
Digital archives face a paradox: they promise permanent access but depend on maintained servers, updated formats, and continued funding — digital resources can disappear faster than physical ones

class_schedule.topic: Building a Digital Humanities Project: Design, Methods, and Ethics
learning_objectives: Design a small-scale digital humanities project with a clear research question
Select appropriate computational methods for a humanities research question
Address the ethical dimensions of digital humanities work: data, access, representation, and labor
readings: Posner, 'How Did They Make That? (DH project analysis framework)'
Sample DH project proposals (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: A digital humanities project is not a technology demonstration. It begins with a research question that computation can help answer — and it must address the ethical dimensions of working with cultural data.
key_concepts: Research question first: the technology serves the question, not the reverse
Method selection: matching computational tools to humanities research goals
Data creation: the labor-intensive process of preparing texts for computational analysis
Ethics of digital humanities: consent, representation, labor, and credit
Project sustainability: planning for long-term access and maintenance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: students pitch DH project ideas and receive feedback on research question clarity, method appropriateness, and feasibility

review.value: The most common DH mistake is starting with a tool and looking for something to do with it; effective projects start with a question that computation can uniquely help answer

The word 'data' means 'things given,' but in DH, data is always constructed through human decisions about what to record, how to categorize it, and what to leave out — every dataset is an interpretation

questions.question: What is 'distant reading' as proposed by Franco Moretti, and how does it differ from the traditional literary critical practice of close reading?
questions.answer: Distant reading analyzes literature at the macro scale — thousands of texts using computational methods like text mining and network analysis — rather than reading individual texts closely. Close reading examines a single text in detail; distant reading seeks patterns across entire literary systems. Moretti argues that close reading can only cover a tiny fraction of literary production and that understanding literature as a system requires methods that operate at a scale beyond individual human reading.

rubric.key: Explains Moretti's rationale for the method

questions.question: What is the 'hermeneutic gap' in digital humanities, and why does it pose a challenge for computational literary analysis?
questions.answer: The hermeneutic gap is the interpretive distance between a computational result and a literary argument. A computer can identify that a word appears with unusual frequency or that two texts are stylistically similar, but it cannot explain what those findings mean. Bridging the gap requires human interpretation, and critics argue that computational findings often confirm what scholars already knew or produce patterns that resist meaningful interpretation.

rubric.key: Provides a concrete example of the problem
Explains why human interpretation remains necessary

questions.question: Why is digitization not a neutral act of preservation? Give two specific examples of how digitization decisions involve interpretation.
questions.answer: Digitization involves interpretive choices at every stage. First, the selection of what to digitize prioritizes some voices and traditions over others — Project Gutenberg's early reliance on out-of-copyright English-language texts over-represented white male authors. Second, metadata and cataloguing decisions shape what users can find and how: categorizing a work as 'African American literature' vs. 'American literature' affects its visibility and framing.

rubric.key: Argues that digitization involves interpretive choices
First example is specific and convincing
Second example is specific and convincing

questions.question: What does it mean to say 'data is not given' in the digital humanities? Why is this important for DH project design?
questions.answer: Although 'data' etymologically means 'things given,' in DH practice data is always constructed through human decisions: what to record, what categories to use, what to exclude, and how to handle ambiguity. This is important because it means every dataset embeds assumptions and biases that shape the results of analysis. DH project design must document these decisions transparently so that users understand the interpretive choices embedded in the data.

rubric.key: Explains that data is constructed, not found
Identifies specific decisions involved in data construction
Connects to transparency and project design

major_assignments.title: Digital Humanities Project
major_assignments.description: Design and execute a small-scale digital humanities project that applies a computational method to a literary research question. The project must include a clear research question, appropriate methodology, results, and a critical reflection on what the method revealed and what it could not.
objectives: Formulate a research question that computational methods can help answer
Apply a DH tool or method to literary texts with technical competence
Interpret computational results through humanistic analysis
Reflect critically on the method's strengths and limitations
deliverables: Project deliverable: visualization, digital edition, map, or analysis report
2,000-word analytical essay interpreting the results and reflecting on methodology
Project documentation: data sources, tools used, and decisions made

criteria: Question is clear, focused, and answerable through computational methods
Question addresses a genuine literary or cultural problem
Methodology is appropriate to the question
Project scope is realistic for the available time and tools
Computational method is applied correctly
Data preparation is documented and defensible
Results are presented clearly through appropriate visualizations or formats
Technical problems are acknowledged and addressed
Results are interpreted through literary and cultural analysis
Computational findings connect to broader scholarly conversations
Limitations of the method are acknowledged honestly
The project demonstrates that DH produces new knowledge, not just new tools
Presentation is clear, concise, and within the time limit
Research question and results are communicated effectively
Visual aids support the presentation
Student responds thoughtfully to questions
Clear research question driving appropriate computational methodology
Technically competent execution with documented process
Meaningful humanistic interpretation of computational results
Honest critical reflection on what the method reveals and what it misses

dh_project.description: Digital humanities project with analytical essay

tool_labs.description: Hands-on labs with DH tools: text analysis, mapping, and network visualization

quizzes.description: Quizzes on DH methods, debates, and readings

seminar_participation.description: Quality of seminar discussion and peer feedback on projects

final_portfolio.description: Revised project with expanded critical reflection and peer review

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory, especially for lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Project deliverables penalized 10% per day late. Lab work not accepted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Collaboration is encouraged on technical problem-solving. All written analysis and interpretation must be individual work. Collaborative projects require clear attribution of each member's contribution.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All data sources, tools, and code must be cited. Using AI tools for analysis is permitted but must be documented. Submitting AI-generated interpretation as your own writing is not permitted.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop required for every class session. Software installations will be guided in class. No prior programming experience required.
